decomposition_notes: Sizing-driven split. The requirements doc describes a decision-then-execute initiative whose full task count (measurement + decision + first-vertical-slice) would total 14 tasks, well past the ~10-task soft cap. Split at the natural decide-vs-execute seam: G1 owns measurement + the decision document; G2 owns the first vertical slice of whichever path the decision selects. CROSS-GOAL CLAIM ORDERING: Claim G1 first and let its final task (the decision document) land in main BEFORE claiming G2. G2's first task cannot be scoped without G1's recommendation — its acceptance criteria reference the path G1 selects. Within each goal, tasks use array-index dependencies for sibling sequencing.

goals.title: Measure the current test suite and produce a decision document
goals.why: Today the test-suite question is debated on vibes — no one has data on which tests are slow, how often they flake, or what the mock-to-integration ratio looks like per module. Decisions land badly when nobody has the numbers.
goals.what: Capture baseline measurements, build a pain inventory, evaluate three candidate paths (status-quo-tighten / convention-overhaul / parallel-replacement), recommend one, and define the first vertical slice for whichever path is chosen.
goals.description: Decision artifact, not implementation. The output is a committed Markdown decision document that names the recommended path and the first vertical slice. G2 picks up from there.
goals.acceptance_criteria:
Baseline measurements committed to a fixture
Pain inventory captured with concrete examples
Three candidate paths evaluated against the same rubric
A single recommendation is named with rationale
The first vertical slice is specified well enough that G2 can start without re-derivation
pitfalls: Do not skip the measurement pass — the cap of the whole initiative is that decisions need data, not vibes
Do not predetermine the recommendation before the data is in

tasks.title: Capture slowest-20 tests baseline
tasks.description: Run `mix test --trace --slowest 20` (or the framework equivalent) and commit the output as a baseline fixture. Includes wall-clock per test, file path, and tag.
tasks.acceptance_criteria:
Output committed at fixtures/test-suite-baseline-slowest-20.txt
Re-running on the same suite produces equivalent results (within 5%)

manual_tests: Run `mix test --trace --slowest 20` twice and confirm the top-20 ordering and per-test wall-clock are stable within 5%
Open fixtures/test-suite-baseline-slowest-20.txt and confirm each row carries file path, test name, and tag
edge_cases: Timing-dependent variance: a warm BEAM/ETS cache or parallel scheduling can reorder near-tied tests — capture on a quiet machine and note the run conditions
async: true tests report per-test time that overlaps wall-clock — the --slowest figure is CPU time, not elapsed, so don't treat the sum as suite duration
Compilation time on a cold build inflates the first run — warm the build before capturing
testing_strategy.coverage_target: Not applicable — this is a measurement fixture, not code; the coverage bar is that the top-20 list reproduces within 5% on re-run

security_considerations: Minimal security surface: this task only runs the existing test suite and commits its stdout. No new code, no credentials, no external input.
Ensure the committed trace output does not leak any secrets, tokens, or DB URLs that a failing/verbose test might print — scrub before committing.
tasks.patterns_to_follow:
Commit the raw `mix test --trace --slowest N` output verbatim so re-runs diff cleanly — do not reformat or hand-edit
Store under fixtures/ alongside the other baseline artifacts, using the test-suite-baseline-* naming already established by sibling tasks
Record the run environment (machine, seed, MIX_ENV) in a header comment so the 5% reproducibility check is meaningful
Use the project's standard `mix test` entry point and ExUnit tags rather than a bespoke runner
pitfalls: Do not capture on a busy machine or during other CPU load — timing noise will break the 5% reproducibility criterion
Do not hand-edit or pretty-print the output; the verification step diffs it byte-for-byte against a fresh run
Do not confuse ExUnit per-test time (CPU) with suite wall-clock (elapsed) when summarizing
Do not fix or tighten any slow test here — this task only measures; remediation is Path A's concern

tasks.title: Capture mock-to-integration ratio per module
tasks.description: Walk test/ directory; per module, count tests using Mox/stubs vs tests using Repo.checkout/Bypass. Commit the table as a baseline fixture.
tasks.acceptance_criteria:
Table committed at fixtures/test-suite-baseline-mock-ratio.md
Includes one row per top-level module in test/

verification_steps.step_text: Spot-check 3 modules in the table against actual test files

manual_tests: Spot-check 3 modules in the committed table against their actual test files and confirm the Mox/stub vs Repo.checkout/Bypass counts match
Confirm the table has exactly one row per top-level directory under test/
edge_cases: A test that uses both a Mox expectation and Repo.checkout in the same block — decide and document a consistent classification rule (e.g. count by dominant dependency) so counts are reproducible
Setup helpers or ExUnit CaseTemplates that inject Mox/Bypass indirectly won't show a literal call site — grep the support/ case modules too or the ratio undercounts
Test files with zero data/HTTP dependencies (pure unit) belong in neither bucket — give them their own column rather than forcing a mock/integration label
testing_strategy.coverage_target: Not applicable — measurement artifact; validated by the 3-module spot-check matching hand-counted reality

security_considerations: Minimal security surface: this is a static walk of test/ that counts patterns and writes a Markdown table. No code execution, no credentials, no external input.
tasks.patterns_to_follow:
Define the classification rule (what counts as a mock vs an integration test) once at the top of the table so the count is reproducible by another reader
Group by the same top-level module boundaries the test/ tree already uses (mirror lib/ structure)
Recognize the project's real seams: Mox/stubs for mocks; Repo.checkout / Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox and Bypass for integration
Commit as Markdown under fixtures/ following the test-suite-baseline-* naming convention
pitfalls: Do not rely on a naive grep for `mock` — Mox usage shows up as `expect(`, `stub(`, and imported case templates, not the literal word
Do not ignore shared setup in test/support — mocks injected there are invisible at the individual test file level
Do not double-count a test that touches both buckets without a stated tie-break rule
Do not editorialize on which ratio is 'good' here — that judgment belongs to the path-evaluation tasks

tasks.title: Capture flake rate over the last 14 CI days
tasks.description: Scrape recent CI runs (or a representative log archive) and tally tests that flipped pass/fail without code changes. Commit per-test flake count.

verification_steps.step_text: Cross-reference CSV against a sample CI log

manual_tests: Cross-reference the committed CSV against a sampled CI log and confirm the pass/fail flips were recorded correctly
Recompute the stated aggregate flake-rate percentage by hand from the CSV rows and confirm it matches
edge_cases: A real code change between two runs is not a flake — only count pass/fail flips where the commit SHA (or test body) is unchanged, or the rate is inflated by legitimate failures
Reruns and retry-on-failure CI settings mask flakes — a test that passed only on the 2nd attempt is a flake even though the run is green; check per-attempt logs
Missing or truncated CI history over the 14-day window — state the actual sample size rather than extrapolating from a partial archive
Tests deleted or renamed mid-window won't span the full period — key on a stable identifier and note partial-coverage rows
testing_strategy.coverage_target: Not applicable — measurement artifact; validated by the CSV reconciling against sampled CI logs and the aggregate percentage recomputing correctly

security_considerations: Low security surface, with one caveat: CI logs frequently contain secrets, tokens, and environment values in failure output — scrub the scraped data and ensure only test-name/outcome columns land in the committed CSV, never raw log bodies.
tasks.patterns_to_follow:
Define a flake precisely as a pass/fail flip with no intervening code change, and state that definition in the CSV header
Key each row on a stable test identifier (file + test name) so it survives across the 14-day window
Attribute retries correctly: a green-after-retry run counts as a flake for that attempt
Commit as CSV under fixtures/ following the test-suite-baseline-* naming convention, with the aggregate rate stated alongside
pitfalls: Do not count failures that coincide with a code change as flakes — filter on unchanged commit SHA
Do not miss retry-masked flakes: a suite that is green only because CI auto-retried still flaked
Do not paste raw CI log bodies into the fixture — they can leak secrets; keep it to test name and outcome columns
Do not extrapolate a rate from a partial archive without stating the true sample size

tasks.title: Build the pain inventory with concrete examples
tasks.description: Walk through 5-10 specific moments where the current architecture slowed a contributor down — with file paths, PR links, or commit refs as evidence.
tasks.acceptance_criteria:
docs/decisions/test-suite-pain-inventory.md committed
Each item has at least one concrete reference (PR / commit / file)

verification_steps.step_text: Review committed file for concrete references

manual_tests: Review the committed docs/decisions/test-suite-pain-inventory.md and confirm every item carries at least one concrete reference (PR link, commit SHA, or file path)
Follow 2-3 of the cited references and confirm they actually demonstrate the claimed pain point
edge_cases: Anecdotal pain with no traceable artifact — either find a PR/commit/file to anchor it or drop it; unreferenced items fail the acceptance criteria
Stale references to code that has since been refactored away — verify each link still points at live evidence
Over-indexing on one loud incident — aim for 5-10 distinct moments across different modules so the inventory isn't a single war story
testing_strategy.coverage_target: Not applicable — decision-document artifact; the bar is that all 5-10 items are concretely referenced and verifiable

security_considerations: Minimal security surface: this is a documentation task. Ensure cited PR/commit references and pasted snippets do not expose secrets or internal-only URLs that shouldn't live in a committed doc.
tasks.patterns_to_follow:
Follow the existing docs/decisions/ Markdown structure and tone (the sibling path-eval docs are the reference)
Every pain item must anchor to a concrete artifact: a PR link, a commit SHA, or a file path — no vibes-only entries
Write for a reader who wasn't there: state the moment, the cost, and the evidence for each item
Keep it descriptive, not prescriptive — remedies belong in the path-evaluation docs, not the inventory
pitfalls: Do not list pain points without a concrete PR/commit/file reference — that is the explicit acceptance criterion
Do not propose solutions here — this is diagnosis; Paths A/B/C own the remedies
Do not pad to hit a count with duplicate or trivial items; 5-10 substantive, distinct moments beats a long thin list
Do not cite references that have gone stale — verify each link still shows the pain

tasks.title: Evaluate path A: status quo + tighten slow tests
tasks.description: Score Path A against the measurement data. Estimate effort to bring the slowest-20 tests under target.

verification_steps.step_text: Review eval against the data fixtures

manual_tests: Review docs/decisions/test-suite-path-a.md and confirm every claim traces back to a baseline fixture (slowest-20, mock-ratio, flakes-14d) rather than assertion
Confirm the effort estimate to bring the slowest-20 under target is present and broken down, not a single hand-wave number
edge_cases: Some slow tests are slow for irreducible reasons (real DB round-trips, external boundary) — the estimate must separate 'tightenable' from 'inherently slow' or the effort figure is fiction
Tightening a test can trade speed for flake risk — flag any slowest-20 entries that also appear in the flake CSV
Effort estimate sensitivity: state the assumption base so a reviewer can recompute if the slowest-20 list shifts
testing_strategy.coverage_target: Not applicable — evaluation document; the bar is that every judgment is grounded in the committed measurement fixtures

security_considerations: Minimal security surface: this is an analysis document scoring an option against committed data. No code changes and no new attack surface.
tasks.patterns_to_follow:
Score against the SAME rubric used by the Path B and Path C evaluations so the three are comparable
Ground every claim in the baseline fixtures (fixtures/test-suite-baseline-*) rather than opinion
Follow the existing docs/decisions/ Markdown format and section layout used across the path evals
Separate the effort estimate into tightenable vs irreducibly-slow buckets so the number is defensible
pitfalls: Do not evaluate against a different rubric than Paths B and C — the three must be directly comparable
Do not assert speedups without pointing at the slowest-20 fixture data
Do not pre-bias toward or against status-quo; the recommendation is a later task's job
Do not lump inherently-slow integration tests into the 'can be tightened' estimate

tasks.title: Evaluate path B: convention overhaul without replacement
tasks.description: Score Path B (mandate Bypass for HTTP, Repo.checkout for DB, kill mox stubs) against the measurement data.

verification_steps.step_text: Review eval against the data fixtures

manual_tests: Review docs/decisions/test-suite-path-b.md and confirm the mandate-Bypass / mandate-Repo.checkout / kill-Mox-stubs proposal is scored against the mock-ratio and flake fixtures
Confirm the migration cost of converting existing Mox stubs is estimated from the actual per-module counts, not guessed
edge_cases: Some Mox stubs exist precisely to avoid a slow or nondeterministic real dependency — killing them may raise suite time or flake rate; call out where the convention trades one pain for another
Bypass and Repo.checkout require setup that async: true tests may not tolerate — flag modules where the convention forces async: false
A blanket convention has uneven cost across modules — weight the estimate by the mock-ratio fixture, don't average
testing_strategy.coverage_target: Not applicable — evaluation document; the bar is data-grounded scoring against the shared rubric

security_considerations: Minimal security surface: analysis document only. If it references how HTTP boundaries are stubbed, ensure no real endpoint credentials or internal hostnames are embedded in examples.
tasks.patterns_to_follow:
Use the SAME rubric as the Path A and Path C evaluations for comparability
Ground the migration-cost estimate in the mock-to-integration ratio fixture, weighted per module
Name the project's real conventions accurately: Bypass for HTTP, Ecto SQL Sandbox / Repo.checkout for DB, Mox for behaviour mocks
Follow the existing docs/decisions/ Markdown format used by the sibling path evals
pitfalls: Do not use a different scoring rubric than Paths A and C
Do not assume killing all Mox stubs is free — some guard against slow or flaky real dependencies; account for the regression risk
Do not ignore async: true incompatibility that a mandated real-dependency convention can introduce
Do not predetermine the recommendation — this task only scores Path B

tasks.title: Evaluate path C: parallel replacement under test_v2
tasks.description: Score Path C (start a parallel test architecture; migrate module by module) against the measurement data.

verification_steps.step_text: Review eval against the data fixtures

manual_tests: Review docs/decisions/test-suite-path-c.md and confirm the parallel test_v2/ architecture is scored against the same rubric and grounded in the baseline fixtures
Confirm the module-by-module migration plan estimates cost from the actual test/ module inventory
edge_cases: Running two test trees means two `mix test` invocations or a combined alias — the eval must address how CI and `mix test --cover` cover both without double-counting or missing files
Coverage tooling may not aggregate cleanly across test/ and test_v2/ — flag the reporting gap during the transition window
A long-lived parallel tree risks permanent limbo (neither tree fully trusted) — the eval should name an exit/convergence criterion
Shared support/ helpers straddling both trees can drift — call out the duplication risk
testing_strategy.coverage_target: Not applicable — evaluation document; but the eval itself must analyze how combined coverage is preserved across two test trees

security_considerations: Minimal security surface: analysis document only. No new code or dependencies are introduced by evaluating the option.
tasks.patterns_to_follow:
Use the SAME rubric as Paths A and B for direct comparability
Ground the migration estimate in the per-module test inventory from the mock-ratio fixture
Address how the existing `mix test` and `mix test --cover` entry points would run and aggregate across test/ and test_v2/
Follow the existing docs/decisions/ Markdown format, and name a convergence/exit criterion for retiring the old tree
pitfalls: Do not use a different rubric than Paths A and B
Do not gloss over how coverage aggregates across two parallel test trees — that is the sharpest risk of this path
Do not omit an exit criterion; a parallel tree with no convergence plan becomes permanent debt
Do not pick a winner here — scoring only; the recommendation task decides

tasks.title: Write the recommendation and first-vertical-slice spec
tasks.description: Pick one of A/B/C with rationale; name the first vertical slice (specific module to migrate / specific tests to tighten / specific convention to mandate) that G2 will execute.
tasks.acceptance_criteria:
docs/decisions/test-suite-recommendation.md committed
First vertical slice is specific enough that G2 can scope tasks without re-derivation

verification_steps.step_text: Confirm the recommendation names the first vertical slice precisely

manual_tests: Confirm docs/decisions/test-suite-recommendation.md names exactly one of A/B/C with rationale that cites the three path-eval docs
Hand off the first-vertical-slice section to a reader unfamiliar with the initiative and confirm they can scope G2's first task without asking a follow-up question
edge_cases: The first slice is under-specified (names a path but not a concrete module/tests/convention) — G2's blocked first task proves this failed; specify the exact target
The recommendation contradicts the data (picks a path the fixtures argue against) — the rationale must reconcile, not ignore, dissenting measurements
Rationale that restates opinions instead of referencing the path evals — every claim should trace to Path A/B/C scores
testing_strategy.coverage_target: Not applicable — decision document; the bar is that G2 can scope its first task from this doc with zero re-derivation

security_considerations: Minimal security surface: this is the capstone decision document. No code, no credentials — just ensure any referenced file paths or examples carry no secrets.
tasks.patterns_to_follow:
Follow the docs/decisions/ Markdown format established by the pain-inventory and path-eval siblings
Name a single recommendation with rationale that cites test-suite-path-a/b/c.md and the baseline fixtures
Specify the first vertical slice concretely: exact module to migrate (Path C), exact slowest-20 tests to tighten (Path A), or exact convention to mandate on a named module (Path B)
Write the slice so it maps directly onto G2's task list — treat this doc as G2's spec of record
pitfalls: Do not leave the first vertical slice vague — G2's first task is explicitly blocked until this slice is unambiguous
Do not recommend a path the measurement data contradicts without reconciling the rationale
Do not defer the choice or hedge across multiple paths — a single recommendation is the deliverable
Do not re-open the measurement debate here; cite the fixtures and decide

goals.title: Implement the first vertical slice from the test-suite decision
goals.why: The decision document from G1 names a specific first vertical slice. G2 ships it.
goals.what: Execute the first vertical slice as specified in docs/decisions/test-suite-recommendation.md. The exact scope depends on the recommendation: tightening the slowest-20 (Path A), enforcing a convention on a representative module (Path B), or migrating one module under test_v2/ (Path C).
goals.where_context: test/ — exact paths depend on the recommendation
goals.description: BLOCKED on G1. The first task here cannot be scoped until docs/decisions/test-suite-recommendation.md lands in main with the first-vertical-slice spec.
goals.acceptance_criteria:
First vertical slice as specified is implemented
Before/after measurements show the expected delta (faster, fewer mocks, etc.)
No regression in pass rate of the existing suite
Documentation updated to reflect the new convention or layer
pitfalls: Do not deviate from the recommendation's first-slice scope — broader changes belong in follow-up tasks
Do not break the existing `mix test` and `mix test --cover` entry points

tasks.title: Re-read the recommendation and confirm the first-vertical-slice scope
tasks.description: Open docs/decisions/test-suite-recommendation.md, identify the named first slice, and confirm the scope is still applicable (no relevant test changes since G1 landed).
tasks.acceptance_criteria: Notes added to the recommendation doc confirming the slice scope or flagging the need for a recommendation refresh

verification_steps.step_text: Read the slice description against current test/ contents

manual_tests: Read the first-slice description in docs/decisions/test-suite-recommendation.md against the current contents of test/ and confirm the named target still exists and is unchanged
Confirm the added confirmation note either affirms the scope or explicitly flags that a recommendation refresh is needed
edge_cases: Test files named in the slice were renamed, moved, or deleted since G1 landed — the note must catch this and flag a refresh rather than proceeding on a stale target
The slowest-20 ordering (Path A) may have shifted since G1's baseline — re-check that the named tests are still the slow ones
Someone already partially applied the convention (Path B) — confirm the slice isn't already done
testing_strategy.coverage_target: Not applicable — verification/notes task; the bar is a written confirmation that the slice scope is current

security_considerations: Minimal security surface: read-and-annotate task on a committed decision doc. No code execution, no new surface.
tasks.patterns_to_follow:
Append the confirmation as a dated note in the existing recommendation doc rather than editing G1's original text
Check the slice target against live test/ contents, not against G1's snapshot assumptions
Use the same Markdown/section style already in docs/decisions/test-suite-recommendation.md
If the scope drifted, flag a refresh explicitly instead of silently re-scoping
pitfalls: Do not start implementing here — this task only confirms scope and annotates the doc
Do not assume the slice is still valid without checking current test/ contents against it
Do not overwrite or rewrite G1's recommendation text; add a note
Do not silently adjust the slice if it drifted — surface the need for a refresh

tasks.title: Implement the first slice — primary code change
tasks.description: Make the changes called for by the first vertical slice. Exact files depend on the recommendation; the test description there is your scope.
tasks.acceptance_criteria:
Code change as specified is in place
Unit tests pass after the change

unit_tests: Run the affected module's existing ExUnit tests and confirm they pass after the change
Add or adjust tests only as the slice's convention requires (e.g. a Bypass-based test replacing a Mox stub), matching the recommendation's stated pattern
integration_tests: For a Path B/C slice, confirm the converted tests exercise the real boundary (Bypass HTTP or Repo.checkout DB) and still pass under Ecto SQL Sandbox
manual_tests: Run `mix test` for the touched files and confirm green before running the full suite
edge_cases: A test converted from a mock to a real dependency may need async: false — verify it doesn't introduce cross-test data bleed under the sandbox
Tightening a slow test (Path A) must not weaken its assertions — confirm it still fails when the code under test is broken
Migrating a module under test_v2/ must not orphan it from the `mix test` entry point — confirm it's actually run
testing_strategy.coverage_target: Coverage for the affected module holds at or above its pre-change level; the full-suite coverage check is a separate downstream task

security_considerations: This task touches test code, not production paths, so the surface is limited to test config: if the slice introduces Bypass or real HTTP stubs, ensure no live credentials, real endpoints, or secrets are hardcoded into test setup.
If a converted test reads env/config, keep it pointed at test-env values so it can't accidentally hit a real service.
tasks.patterns_to_follow:
Follow the first-vertical-slice spec in docs/decisions/test-suite-recommendation.md exactly — it is your scope of record
Mirror the existing test conventions for the chosen path: Bypass for HTTP, Ecto SQL Sandbox / Repo.checkout for DB, Mox only where the convention retains it
Keep async: true where the converted dependency allows it; drop to async: false only when the real boundary requires isolation
Match the surrounding module's ExUnit case template, tags, and setup style rather than inventing a new structure
pitfalls: Do not exceed the first-slice scope — broader changes belong in follow-up slices, per the goal pitfalls
Do not break the existing `mix test` / `mix test --cover` entry points
Do not weaken assertions while tightening a slow test — a faster test that no longer catches regressions is a net loss
Do not introduce cross-test data bleed by converting to a real dependency without correct sandbox/async settings
Do not hardcode real credentials or endpoints into converted test setup

tasks.title: Capture before/after measurement against the slice's target metric
tasks.description: Run the same measurement procedure G1 ran for the affected module; commit the after-figure.

verification_steps.step_text: Confirm before/after delta matches the recommendation's predicted delta

manual_tests: Re-run the exact measurement procedure G1 used for the affected module (slowest-N, mock count, or flake check) and confirm the after-figure is captured with the same method
Confirm fixtures/test-suite-slice-after.md presents before and after side by side with the delta computed
edge_cases: Measurement method drift: if you measure the after-figure differently than G1 measured the before-figure, the delta is meaningless — reuse the identical command and conditions
Timing noise on the after-run: capture on a comparably quiet machine, matching the baseline's run conditions
A delta that misses the recommendation's prediction is a real signal, not a number to massage — record it honestly and let the follow-up task react
testing_strategy.coverage_target: Not applicable — measurement artifact; validated by the before/after delta being computed with the same method G1 used

security_considerations: Minimal security surface: re-runs an existing measurement and commits a Markdown table. Scrub any secrets from captured trace/log output before committing, same as the G1 baseline tasks.
tasks.patterns_to_follow:
Reuse the EXACT measurement procedure and command G1 ran for this module so before and after are comparable
Pull the 'before' figure from the committed baseline fixture rather than re-deriving it
Commit under fixtures/ following the test-suite-* naming convention, matching the baseline artifacts' format
Present before, after, and delta together, and state the run conditions
pitfalls: Do not measure the after-figure with a different method or command than the baseline used — the comparison breaks
Do not capture on a noisy machine; timing variance can swamp the real delta
Do not massage or cherry-pick the after-figure to match the predicted delta — record it honestly
Do not re-derive the 'before' number; take it from the committed G1 baseline fixture

tasks.title: Confirm no regression in pass rate on the existing suite
tasks.description: Run `mix test --cover` end-to-end; verify pass rate and coverage match baseline.
tasks.acceptance_criteria:
mix test --cover passes
Coverage unchanged or improved relative to baseline

verification_steps.expected_result: All tests pass; coverage at/above baseline

manual_tests: Run `mix test --cover` end-to-end and confirm all tests pass
Compare the reported coverage percentage against the pre-slice baseline and confirm it is unchanged or improved
edge_cases: A newly flaky test can mask as a regression on a single run — re-run before concluding the slice caused a failure, and consult the flake baseline
Coverage can dip legitimately if the slice deleted redundant tests — distinguish a real coverage loss from removing dead test code, and document which
A slice that moved tests under test_v2/ must confirm those tests are actually included in the `mix test` run, or coverage looks fine only because they aren't executing
testing_strategy.coverage_target: Full-suite coverage unchanged or improved relative to the pre-slice baseline; all tests pass under `mix test --cover`

security_considerations: Minimal security surface: runs the existing suite with coverage. No code changes and no new surface introduced by this verification task.
tasks.patterns_to_follow:
Use the project's standard `mix test --cover` entry point exactly — do not substitute a partial or filtered run
Compare against the recorded baseline coverage figure rather than an eyeballed threshold
If a failure appears, check it against the flake baseline before attributing it to the slice
Follow the project's coverage-threshold expectations from AGENTS.md
pitfalls: Do not run only the affected files — a regression check must cover the whole suite
Do not declare a regression on a single red run without re-running to rule out a known flake
Do not accept a silent coverage drop caused by test_v2/ tests not being wired into `mix test`
Do not lower the coverage bar to make the check pass

tasks.title: Update CONTRIBUTING / AGENTS notes to reflect the new convention
tasks.description: Adjust the project's contributor docs so the next contributor knows about the new pattern or layering established by the slice.
tasks.acceptance_criteria:
AGENTS.md or CONTRIBUTING.md updated
The new convention is named explicitly

manual_tests: Re-read the updated section in AGENTS.md / CONTRIBUTING.md and confirm the new convention is named explicitly, not merely alluded to
Have a reader unfamiliar with the slice follow the new note and confirm they'd write the next test the intended way
edge_cases: The new convention may contradict an older testing note already in AGENTS.md — reconcile or remove the stale guidance rather than leaving two conflicting rules
Convention stated too abstractly to act on — include a concrete before/after example from the slice
Documenting a convention the slice only partially rolled out — scope the note to what's actually true today, not the aspirational end state
testing_strategy.coverage_target: Not applicable — documentation task; the bar is that the new convention is named explicitly and a contributor can act on it

security_considerations: Minimal security surface: contributor-documentation edit. No executable content; just ensure any example snippets carry no secrets or real endpoints.
tasks.patterns_to_follow:
Follow the existing AGENTS.md testing-guidelines structure and voice — slot the convention alongside the current quality/testing rules
Name the convention explicitly and give a concrete example drawn from the first slice
Reconcile against any pre-existing testing guidance so the docs don't contradict themselves
Keep the note scoped to what the slice actually established, not the full future migration
pitfalls: Do not describe the convention vaguely — the acceptance criterion is that it is named explicitly
Do not leave stale, now-contradictory testing guidance in place alongside the new note
Do not document an aspirational end state the slice hasn't reached
Do not duplicate the whole recommendation doc here; link to it and state the actionable rule

tasks.title: Define follow-up slices and update the recommendation doc
tasks.description: Based on what worked and what surprised in the first slice, define the next 2-3 vertical slices in the recommendation doc.
tasks.acceptance_criteria:
docs/decisions/test-suite-recommendation.md gets a 'Follow-up slices' section
Each next-slice item is specific enough to become a Stride task

verification_steps.step_text: Confirm each next slice can be scoped without further discussion

manual_tests: Confirm docs/decisions/test-suite-recommendation.md gains a 'Follow-up slices' section with 2-3 concrete next items
For each next slice, confirm it could be pasted into a Stride task with a scoped target and no further discussion needed
edge_cases: A follow-up slice that repeats the first slice's surprises without adapting — the 'what worked / what surprised' learnings from slice one must actually shape the next items
Slices that are too large to be a single ~1-3h task — split them so each maps to one Stride work task
Vague next steps ('improve more modules') that fail the 'scopable without discussion' bar — name the specific module/tests/convention each time
testing_strategy.coverage_target: Not applicable — planning document; the bar is that each follow-up slice is concrete enough to become a Stride task directly

security_considerations: Minimal security surface: appends a planning section to a committed decision doc. No code, no credentials.
tasks.patterns_to_follow:
Append a 'Follow-up slices' section in the existing docs/decisions/test-suite-recommendation.md format rather than starting a new doc
Specify each next slice with the same concreteness the first-slice spec required: exact module/tests/convention as the target
Feed the first slice's before/after result and surprises into the sizing and choice of the next items
Size each slice so it maps onto a single Stride work task (~1-3 hours, complexity small)
pitfalls: Do not write vague follow-ups like 'do more modules' — each must be scopable into a Stride task without further discussion
Do not ignore what the first slice taught; the follow-ups should reflect the measured result, not the original guesses
Do not bundle a multi-day effort into one slice — split to task-sized units
Do not start implementing any follow-up here; this task only defines them
